key_insight: A fair, apples-to-apples comparison shows that the best inverse-solver choice depends mainly on how the unknown is represented: discrete adjoints excel for grid-based fields, while PINNs are more natural for neural/functional representations.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained inverse problems in mechanics and fluids, the paper clarifies when to use adjoint optimization, when PINNs are competitive, and how to combine them for lower-cost high-accuracy recovery.
method: Common-formulation benchmark with matched domains, PDEs, observation models, regularization, optimizer, unknown parameterization, and precision.
Compared adjoint optimization and PINNs on unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy inversion, 3D Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and unsteady Navier--Stokes viscosity identification.
Proposed a PINN-warm-started adjoint workflow to retain adjoint-level accuracy at reduced computational cost.
evidence_signal: The evaluation spans four PDE-constrained inverse benchmarks and compares reconstruction quality and cost under matched problem settings.
watch_item: Results depend strongly on the chosen parameterization and, for time-dependent problems, adjoint methods can be limited by trajectory storage and differentiation overhead.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
